papers.title: Hypervelocity star candidates from Gaia DR2 and DR3 proper motions and parallaxes

abstract.text: Hypervelocity stars (HVSs) unbound to the Galaxy can be formed with extreme stellar interactions. Observational evidence comes from measurements of radial velocities (RVs) of objects crossing the Galactic halo and of tangential velocities based on high proper motions (HPMs) and distances of relatively nearby stars. I searched for new HVS candidates and reviewed known objects using their Gaia astrometric measurements. Candidates were selected with significant Gaia parallaxes of >0.1mas, proper motions of >20mas/yr, and computed Galactocentric tangential velocities $vtan\\\\_g$>500km/s. The DR2 and DR3 samples of several thousand HVS candidates were studied with respect to their proper motions, sky distribution, number of observations, location in crowded fields, colour-magnitude diagrams, selection effects with magnitude, and RVs in DR3. The most extreme ($vtan\\\\_g$>700km/s) and nearest (within 4kpc) 72 DR3 HVS candidates were investigated with respect to detected close neighbours, flags and astrometric quality parameters of objects of similar magnitudes in DR3. The quality checks involved HPM objects in a global comparison and all objects in the vicinity of each target. Spurious HPMs in the Galactic centre region led to false HVS interpretations in Gaia DR2 and are still present in DR3, although to a lesser extent. Otherwise there is good agreement between the HPMs of HVS candidates in DR2 and DR3. However, HVS candidates selected from DR2 tend to have larger parallaxes hence lower tangential velocities in DR3. Most DR3 RVs are much lower than the tangential velocities, indicating that the DR3 HVS candidates are still affected by underestimated parallaxes. None of the 72 extreme nearby DR3 HVS candidates, including three D$^6$ stars, passed all quality checks. Their tangential velocities may turn out to be smaller, but at least some of them still appear unbound to the Galaxy. (abbreviated)

brief.tldr: [research paper] The study critically evaluates hypervelocity star (HVS) candidates from Gaia DR2 and DR3, identifying systematic biases in astrometric data—particularly spurious high proper motions (HPMs) in the Galactic center region and underestimated parallaxes—that lead to inflated tangential velocities. Despite improved data in DR3, most HVS candidates exhibit significantly lower radial velocities than their computed tangential velocities, suggesting uncorrected distance errors; among 72 extreme nearby candidates, none pass rigorous quality checks involving astrometric consistency and nearby neighbor analysis, indicating potential false positives or underestimation of binding energies.

source_fields.title: Hypervelocity star candidates from Gaia DR2 and DR3 proper motions and parallaxes

source_fields.sections_text: Introduction: This study introduces a critical review of astrometric measurements—particularly high proper motions and parallaxes—to assess the validity of claimed hypervelocity stars, addressing the problem of potential overestimation of tangential velocities due to observational uncertainties and systematic errors in distance determinations, thereby improving the reliability of escape velocity assessments for Galactic halo stars. Summary and conclusions: This study highlights the uncertainty in Gaia parallaxes and proper motions, particularly in crowded regions, leading to spurious hypervelocity star candidates, and recommends increasing the minimum number of observations ($N_{\\text{per}}$) to improve astrometric accuracy and reduce false positives in HVS selections. Gaia HVS selection from tangential velocities: This section describes the selection and refinement of hypervelocity star (HVS) candidates from Gaia data by applying conservative tangential velocity cuts in the galactocentric rest frame, with initial thresholds of >500 km/s and later tightened to >700 km/s for the most promising candidates, emphasizing the impact of parallax errors and astrometric quality on velocity measurements. Verification of nearest extreme HVS candidates: The verification of the nearest extreme HVS candidates reveals that only a small fraction of the most extreme HVS candidates have high astrometric quality and plausible HVS status, with most lacking radial velocity support and potential contamination from companions or crowded fields undermining their reliability.

abstract: Hypervelocity stars (HVSs) unbound to the Galaxy can be formed with extreme stellar interactions.

evidence comes from measurements of radial velocities (RVs) of objects crossing the Galactic halo and of tangential velocities based on high proper motions (HPMs) and distances of relatively nearby stars
None of the 72 extreme nearby DR3 HVS candidates, including three D$^6$ stars, passed all quality checks.
title: Hypervelocity star candidates from Gaia DR2 and DR3 proper motions and parallaxes

catalog_assessment.evidence: Title and abstract explicitly describe selection of HVS candidates from Gaia DR2/DR3 using astrometric cuts, analysis of 'several thousand' candidates, and detailed study of 72 extreme objects. Paper presents object-level data selection and quality assessment.

papers.title: High-speed stars. II. An unbound star, young stars, bulge metal-poor stars, and Aurora candidates
papers.author_names: P. Bonifacio E. Caffau L. Monaco L. Sbordone M. Spite A. Mucciarelli P. François L. Lombardo A.D.M. Matas Pinto

abstract.text: The data from the Gaia satellite led us to revise our conception of the Galaxy structure and history. Hitherto unknown components have been discovered and a deep re-thinking of what the Galactic halo is is in progress. We selected from the Gaia catalogue stars with extreme transverse velocities with respect to the Sun ($|V_T| > 500 $ and observed them with FORS2 at the ESO VLT, to classify them using both their chemical and dynamical properties. Two apparently young stars, identified in paper\\\\,I, were observed with UVES. We derived abundances for Na, Mg, Ca, Ti, Mn, and Fe, analysing the spectra with while for Ba we used line profile fitting. We computed actions from parallaxes and kinematical data. The stars span the metallicity range $ Fe/H -0.5$ with $ Fe/H = -1.6$. Star GHS143 has a total speed of about 1440 which is almost three times faster than the local escape velocity of 522 strongly implying this star is unbound to the Galaxy. Remarkably, this star is not escaping from the Galaxy, but it is falling into it. Ten stars are apparently young with masses in excess of 1.3M. Their interpretation as evolved blue stragglers is doubtful. The existence of a young metal-poor population is possible. The two stars observed with UVES show no lithium, suggesting they are blue stragglers. We detected a metal-poor population, confined to the bulge, that we call SpiteF, and argue that it is the result of a recent accretion event. We detect 102 candidates of the Aurora population that should have formed prior to the formation of the disc. Our sample is non-homogeneous and mainly retrograde. The stars are metal poor, and 23<!PCT!> have Fe/H -2.0$. Our selection is efficient at finding very metal-poor stars, but it selects peculiar populations.

triage.label: rule-direct
triage.reason: Direct title match: unbound, escaping, or ejected stars

brief.tldr: [research paper] The study identifies a sample of high-velocity halo stars from Gaia data, revealing an unbound star (GHS143) with a total speed of ~1440 km/s—over three times the local escape velocity—indicating it is falling into the Galaxy rather than escaping. Ten stars show young, high-mass characteristics inconsistent with blue straggler evolution, suggesting a possible young metal-poor population. A metal-poor bulge population (SpiteF) is linked to a recent accretion event, while 102 Aurora candidates—stars formed prior to disc formation—are detected, challenging prior claims that the halo consists only of thick disc or GSE-derived stars. The sample is retrograde and non-homogeneous, with 23% having [Fe/H] ≤ −2.0, highlighting the complexity of Galactic halo formation through accretion and early collapse phases.

source_fields.title: High-speed stars. II. An unbound star, young stars, bulge metal-poor stars, and Aurora candidates

source_fields.sections_text: Discussion: The study finds that extreme kinematic stars are not necessarily chemically extreme, with a peak in metallicity around [Fe/H] ≈ –1.4 and a clear bifurcation in the [Mg/Fe] vs [Fe/H] diagram at [Fe/H] ≈ –1.5 dex primarily due to GSE and bulge stars, identifying four key stellar populations: an unbound star, young stars, metal-poor bulge stars, and Aurora candidates. Kinematics: The stellar kinematics are analyzed using Gaia DR3 data with zero-point-corrected parallaxes, standard Galactic model assumptions, and careful uncertainty propagation, confirming the bound status of most stars and identifying GHS143 as unbound, with distance and velocity uncertainties rigorously evaluated. Conclusions: The study concludes that stars with high transverse velocities are a heterogeneous group, primarily consisting of halo stars with only a few linked to past accretion events, including potential evidence for a recent accretion structure like SpiteF, while also revealing a surprising population of massive, likely young stars that challenge current interpretations of blue stragglers and warrant further high-resolution observational follow-up. Introduction: This paper addresses the open question of whether the Milky Way's halo contains stars formed during the early Galactic collapse (the "Aurora" population) by analyzing kinematic and chemical properties of metal-poor halo stars, proposing a novel distinction between stars formed in accreted dwarf galaxies and those originating from the early Galactic collapse. Target selection: The study selects high-transverse-velocity stars from Gaia catalogues, initially focusing on blue, metal-poor stars and later expanding to redder stars to obtain a representative metallicity distribution, using PARSEC isochrones to contextualize stellar ages and metallicities. Chemical analysis: This section presents the determination of stellar parameters, including effective temperature, surface gravity, and metallicity, for a sample of stars using iterative photometric and spectroscopic analysis, with particular attention to young and metal-poor stars. Appendix A Target stars: This section lists the names and Gaia DR3 identifiers of 120 target stars observed in the study. Appendix B ADQL query used: The ADQL query identifies high transverse speed stars from Gaia DR3 with proper motion exceeding 500 mas/yr and photometric magnitude between 7 and 13. Appendix D Clustering analysis: A clustering analysis of high-speed stars using E, LZ, and JR parameters reveals seven distinct groups, with two clusters (4 and 6) associated with Galactic Stream (GSE) and bulge stars, as identified through HDBSCAN and validated via t-SNE projections. Observations and radial velocities: This section describes the FORS and UVES observations of 350 stars, including radial velocity measurements derived from template matching and compared to Gaia RVS, with FORS velocities showing a small systematic offset but good correlation, and highlights the use of lithium abundance as a diagnostic to distinguish blue stragglers from young stars. Appendix E SpiteF: Further discussion: The SpiteF structure is confirmed as a distinct group of metal-poor bulge stars with [Fe/H] ≈ −2.0 to −2.5 and [Mg/Fe] ratios consistent with low-metallicity populations, with 15 out of 16 SpiteF stars also belonging to the RZ sample confined to rₐₚ < 5.4 kpc and Zₘₐₓ < 3.8 kpc, and kinematic properties indicating a coherent inner Galactic distribution. Appendix C Remarks on individual stars: This section discusses the identification and analysis of a highly reddened B-type star (GHS076) with significant interstellar extinction and variable stars in the sample, including one classified as a δ Scuti/CEMP star. Appendix F Limitations of MyGIsFOS for ionised species: MyGIsFOS provides unreliable quantitative abundance estimates for ionized species or features sensitive to electron density, such as Ba ii, when the abundance exceeds [Ba/Fe] ≈ 0.3, due to the significant metallicity-dependent variations in electron density across model atmospheres.

abstract: Two apparently young stars, identified in paper\\\\,I, were observed with UVES.
The stars span the metallicity range $ Fe/H -0.5$ with $ Fe/H = -1.6$.
Ten stars are apparently young with masses in excess of 1.3M. Their interpretation as evolved blue stragglers is doubtful. The existence of a young metal-poor population is possible.
The two stars observed with UVES show no lithium, suggesting they are blue stragglers.
The stars are metal poor, and 23 have Fe/H -2.0$. Our selection is efficient at finding very metal-poor stars, but it selects peculiar populations.
title: High-speed stars. II. An unbound star, young stars, bulge metal-poor stars, and Aurora candidates

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper describes selection of high-velocity stars from Gaia and follow-up spectroscopic observations with FORS2/UVES at VLT. Provides derived abundances, kinematics, and identifies specific objects (e.g., GHS143) and populations (Aurora candidates).

papers.title: Where do they come from? Identification of globular cluster escaped stars
papers.author_names: Cheng Xu Baitian Tang Chengyuan Li José G. Fernández-Trincado Jing Zhong Long Wang Hao Tian Yang Huang

abstract.text: Globular clusters (GCs), as old as our Galaxy, constantly lose their members to the field as they cross through the Milky Way (MW). These GC escaped stars (or escapees) are suggested to contribute significantly to the MW halo. If a star has left the host GC a long time ago, chemical finger prints, e.g., N enrichment, may reveal its origin. In this work, we aim to establish dynamical connections between N-rich field stars recently identified by LAMOST and the existing MW GCs. By constructing the full action distribution, and combining with metallicity, we found 29 potential GC progenitors for 15 N-rich field stars. Particularly, some of them may be related to MW accretion events. On the other hand, if a star recently left its host GC via tidal evaporation, it still maintain the kinematic properties of the cluster. Here we identify extra-tidal candidates based on their spatial locations, proper motions (PMs), and their position on color-magnitude-diagrams (CMDs). We successfully identified more than 1600 extra-tidal candidates in the vicinity of six Gaia-Enceladus (GE)-related GCs (i.e., NGC 1851, NGC 1904, NGC 6205, NGC 6341, NGC 6779, NGC 7089). The density map of the extra-tidal candidates is confirmed to be an efficient way to find extra-tidal structures. The possible two density peaks at opposite directions of the inner boundary is a good indicator for long stellar stream. Among 95 extra-tidal candidates with spectroscopic radial velocities and metallicity, 54 of them are confirmed to be GC escaped stars, as they share similar properties as host GCs. These extra-tidal candidates are ideal targets for follow-up spectroscopic observation, as it greatly improves the scientific outcome. Once statistically significant number of spectroscopic radial velocities and metallicities are available, the GC dynamical evolution (e.g., mass loss, rotation) can be carefully investigated.

triage.label: rule-weak-llm-confirmed
triage.reason: Weak title match: stellar escaper candidates LLM confirmed: Paper focuses on 'GC escaped stars' and 'extra-tidal candidates' - stars that have left globular clusters, which fits the definition of high-velocity/runaway stars escaping from stellar systems.

brief.skipped_reason: weak rule match; retained with search metadata only

source_fields.sections_text: Methods: The paper proposes a method that identifies host globular clusters (GCs) of N-rich field stars by comparing their integrals of motion—such as orbital energy, angular momentum, and actions—assuming both the Milky Way potential is axisymmetric and steady-state, and that escaped stars retain kinematic and metallicity properties similar to their GC progenitors. Summary: This study identifies N-rich field stars and extra-tidal candidates as escaped stars from globular clusters, using chemo-dynamical and phase-space analyses to link them to GCs and reveal evidence of tidal evaporation and accretion events in the Milky Way. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel method to identify Galactic Center (GC) escapees by combining kinematic and chemical abundance analyses of stellar streams, addressing the problem of tracing the origin and evolution of GCs in the Milky Way's halo through the detection of tidally stripped stars with host-GC-like properties. Results and discussion: The experiment found that 29 out of 137 N-rich field star–GC pairs have a matching probability $p > 0.32$, with 16 showing consistent metallicities, and most of these associations likely stem from accretion events such as Gaia-Enceladus or the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy. Appendix A Orbits of 6 GCs in Sec [3.2](#S3.SS2 "3.2 Extra-tidal candidates around clusters ‣ 3 Results and discussion ‣ Where do they come from? Identification of globular cluster escaped stars"): The experiment found that NGC 1851 and NGC 6779 exhibit the most pronounced stellar orbits with high velocity dispersions, achieving peak performance metrics of 12.3 km/s and 14.7 km/s, respectively, indicating stronger dynamical activity compared to other globular clusters.

abstract: These GC escaped stars (or escapees) are suggested to contribute significantly to the MW halo.
In this work, we aim to establish dynamical connections between N-rich field stars recently identified by LAMOST and the existing MW GCs.
By constructing the full action distribution, and combining with metallicity, we found 29 potential GC progenitors for 15 N-rich field stars.
Among 95 extra-tidal candidates with spectroscopic radial velocities and metallicity, 54 of them are confirmed to be GC escaped stars, as they share similar properties as host GCs.
title: Identification of globular cluster escaped stars

catalog_assessment.evidence: Identifies >1600 extra-tidal (GC escaped) candidates around six globular clusters using spatial, proper motion, and CMD criteria. Confirms 54 with spectroscopy. Presents object-level candidate lists and dynamical connections.
